issue_title: Fp8 scaled-mm row-wise is substantially slower than tensor-wise
surface_reasoning: Priority 3: labeled `module: performance` + `module: cuda` + `module: floatx (formerly float8)`. Title: 'Fp8 scaled-mm row-wise is substantially slower than tensor-wise' — dispatch bug where fp8 row-wise scaling matmul underperforms tensor-wise scaling. Domain relevance: fp8 scaled matmul uses specialized CUDA tensor core kernels (e.g., cuBLAS with fp8 support); row-wise vs tensor-wise scaling requires different kernel invocations and the slowness indicates wrong dispatch.
confidence_justification: Start: 0.50. Step 1: No 'module: correctness (silent)' label — no +0.25. Step 2: No maintainer approved_fix or requested_merge signal — no bonus. Step 3: No self-contained repro script (benchmarks shown as images and gist link; no standalone Python code block in the issue body). Partial signal: gist link provided and quantitative benchmark data present → +0.05 (partial repro, not full +0.15). Running total: 0.55. Step 4: 'triaged' label present → +0.10. Running total: 0.65. Step 5: fix_scope = single_module (CUTLASS row-wise kernel within floatx module) — not single_function/single_file, no +0.10. Step 6: Issue open ~1.2 years (Feb 2025 to Apr 2026) — does not meet 2+ year threshold; no +0.05. Step 7: No blocking signals. No wont_fix, blocked_action, or rejected PR. still_reproducible = 'likely'. No negative adjustments. Final: 0.65. Reducing to 0.60: the issue is explicitly framed as uncertain ('unclear whether this is a substantial limitation or suboptimal implementation'), and the fix likely requires deep CUTLASS kernel expertise making it less accessible to typical contributors. The author themselves (a domain expert) has already worked on this and filed it as a tracking issue rather than a fix attempt.
ground_truth_delta: Like ambiguous_72408, this is a real performance issue with detailed benchmarks but uncertain actionability. Key differences: (1) no maintainer has said 'no real way to act on this' — the blocked_action hard blocker is absent; (2) the performance gap is 0-50% (row-wise vs tensor-wise efficiency) rather than 30x slowdown in 72408; (3) the author already improved the fast_accum case via PR #134781 and explicitly frames this as a follow-up optimization task, suggesting it is more actionable than 72408's external-lib-dependency blocker; (4) the fix is in CUTLASS kernel tuning rather than requiring an entirely new library integration; (5) the issue is only ~1.2 years old with no maintainer engagement. More actionable than 72408 but still performance-only with no correctness label and high specialist knowledge bar.
